topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. I peered through the peephole, my jaw tightening as I recognized the delivery person. It was a box, clearly addressed to the previous occupants of Apartment 3B. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my chest. I retrieved the package, the cardboard feeling cheap and insulting in my hands. When I finally wrestled the door open, the sight of the contents—a collection of dusty, outdated kitchen gadgets—sent a sharp, involuntary flinch through me. I set it down on the welcome mat with exaggerated care, my voice clipped and low. "Excuse me," I managed, the word tasting like grit. "This isn't for me."

[story 2]

I found the package tucked behind the planter box, a baffling piece of mail left carelessly on my doorstep. My brow furrowed immediately; who would send something to me? It was clearly marked with an old forwarding address. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I picked it up, the paper crinkling under my fingers with unnecessary force. As I examined the flimsy contents—a stack of old tax documents, perhaps—a distinct hardening settled in my shoulders. I walked to the recycling bin, deliberately placing the item near the top, making sure it was visible. "Well," I said, my tone carefully neutral, though my eyes held a sharp, unyielding glare toward the hallway. "This is clearly misplaced."

[story 3]

The delivery arrived just as I was attempting to unpack my own belongings. A parcel sat squarely on the threshold, bearing the faded lettering of a former resident. A strange, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I stared at it, my hands instinctively curling into fists at my sides. It felt like a deliberate slight, a message sent directly to me. When I finally moved to take the box inside, every movement was stiff, deliberate. I didn't bother to open it right away. Instead, I placed it on the dining table, angled so that it dominated the space, a silent declaration of my displeasure hanging heavy in the air between us.
